turns.content: I'm onboarding to a new embedded project and I keep seeing #[panic_handler] everywhere. It feels like the Rust standard library panic mechanism, but it's not quite the same since this is no_std land where there are no global destructors or main entry points.

That's a great observation. In std environments panics propagate through unwinding and eventually hit the standard library, but in no_std you need to define your own panic handler that takes an impl PanicHandler trait argument. The compiler generates a call site for this handler whenever a panic occurs so the program doesn't just stop abruptly on bare metal.
So if I want to log the panic message instead of crashing immediately, how do I wire that up? Do I need some specific crate like panic-probe or something?

You can use panic-probe which provides a simple way to register a callback that runs before the actual halt. But in many embedded systems people prefer defmt logging because it's lightweight and efficient. You'd define your own #[panic_handler] block where you call defmt::write!() with the message string, then let defmt handle the formatting without allocating.
What happens if I try to panic in a function that doesn't have any stack space available? Will the panic handler itself consume memory?

Good catch. If you're trying to log via heap allocation, you'll fail because there's no allocator in no_std environments until you bootstrap one. That's why defmt is so popular here—it formats messages without any dynamic memory allocations at all, making it safe for zero-allocation scenarios.

Ah, I see! So the panic handler becomes a way to handle fatal errors gracefully by logging them instead of just halting. That's like how my production system logs errors before shutting down, but here it's happening at compile time and runtime without needing extra heap.

Exactly. Think of the panic handler as your system's emergency log that captures critical failures before termination. It turns what could be a silent crash into a diagnosable event, which is crucial when you can't afford to lose data on bare metal.
Hey, I'm new to this job and the code is all no_std. I keep seeing panic handlers everywhere but my IDE crashes if I try to run it like a normal program. What's going on?

That's totally expected when you switch from std to no_std environments. In no_std, there is no runtime stack or standard library to catch exceptions the way your IDE does in desktop apps. Instead of catching panics automatically, the panic handler becomes the actual entry point for error handling on embedded systems.
So if I call println and it panics, my program just dies? That seems risky compared to how normal Rust programs work with try blocks or result types.

Exactly, which is why you need a panic handler. Without one, the process halts immediately on any panic. In no_std contexts like microcontrollers, you use #[panic_handler] to define custom behavior instead of letting it crash. You can log messages via defmt or use crates like panic-probe to handle errors gracefully.
But wait, shouldn't we just avoid panics entirely? I thought Rust was supposed to be safe from crashes?

You're right that Rust aims for safety through ownership and lifetimes. But in no_std, you still need explicit panic handlers because there's no global exception mechanism like std::panic::catch_unwind. The panic handler lets you choose whether to log via defmt or just halt the system entirely depending on your safety requirements.
So if I want to avoid panics completely, I should use try blocks everywhere? But what about when something really bad happens like a hardware failure?

For normal logic errors, yes, you can use try blocks and let the compiler handle it. But for hardware failures or unrecoverable bugs, panics are sometimes intentional because they trigger immediate shutdowns that might be safer than continuing with corrupted state. The panic handler determines whether those crashes become fatal or get logged instead.
I see. So defmt is the logging mechanism here? Is it faster than println?

Yes, defmt is designed specifically for embedded systems where you want zero-cost logging without allocating memory. Unlike println which requires heap allocation and parsing, defmt messages are parsed directly by the hardware at compile time using traits like defmt::Format.
That sounds efficient but what if I need to log more complex data structures? Can I still use them with defmt?

You can define custom traits for complex types so they implement the required trait interface. For example, you might create a struct that implements defmt::Format and then log it alongside simpler values like usize or String without worrying about allocation overhead.
Wait, but what if I don't want to panic at all? Can I disable panics entirely?

You can set the panic handler to halt without logging or use no-alloc panic messages that avoid dynamic allocation. But you still need some form of panic handling in no_std because Rust won't catch panics automatically like it does with std libraries.
Okay, so the key takeaway is that #[panic_handler] gives us control over what happens when things go wrong. Thanks for walking me through this!
You're welcome! Just remember to keep your panic handlers minimal and use defmt whenever possible since it avoids the overhead of println in constrained environments.

I thought since I'm using a panic handler, panics would just print to stderr like in std and then the program would keep running. That way I could catch errors gracefully without crashing my microcontroller.

That's a common misconception when coming from standard Rust environments. In no_std contexts with embedded hardware, panics are far more dangerous because they don't have a default handler to return an error code or print logs safely. Without #[panic_handler], a panic will immediately halt the processor forever since there is no stack unwinding or cleanup phase available in bare-metal setups.

Oh right, so my microcontroller just stops dead? That's frustrating because I was trying to implement a graceful shutdown logic instead of letting it crash. Can't I just catch panics and log them instead?

You can't really 'catch' panics in the same way you do with try blocks or Result types since panic is not an exception but a runtime abort mechanism by design. Instead, you must install #[panic_handler] before your main function runs so that when a panic occurs, it logs the message via defmt or another logging channel and then resets the device to a safe state rather than hanging indefinitely.
Okay, so I need to use panic-probe crate? Is that the only way to do this without allocating memory since my heap is tiny?

The panic-probe crate allows you to recover from a panic by catching it at runtime and executing custom code, but using it does involve some allocation which might be problematic on very constrained systems. For truly no-alloc panics, the standard approach involves implementing #[panic_handler] directly in your crate root or using crates like defmt that provide minimal logging without heap usage.
I see, so if I want to avoid any panic handling overhead, I should just disable panics entirely? That sounds safer than trying to recover from them.

Yes, disabling panics via const fn is often the cleanest approach for safety-critical embedded code where you cannot afford any runtime overhead or unexpected behavior. You can use no-panic macros or carefully structure your logic so that errors are returned as Result types instead of unwinding the stack, ensuring deterministic execution without relying on panic handlers at all.

I've been working a lot with web frameworks where errors just bubble up to the browser or return 500 responses, but when I look at embedded systems I'm wondering what happens if you panic there. It feels like my code should just crash and die immediately, right? Or does it get caught somewhere?

That's a great observation about how web frameworks handle errors versus how bare metal systems behave. In Rust on the web side, the panic unwinds the stack until an error handler is found or the process exits. However, in embedded environments using no_std where there is no standard library and often no operating system to catch exceptions, panics are much more dangerous because they can't be handled by a normal exception mechanism. Instead of bubbling up, a panic will halt execution forever unless you explicitly set up a #[panic_handler] that intercepts the signal before it stops the program.
So I'm imagining this is like my web server catching an error and returning HTML instead of crashing? But if I don't have a handler, does the CPU just stop executing instructions?

Exactly. Without a #[panic_handler], the panic unwinds the stack and terminates the process immediately without any cleanup or logging. This is why many embedded developers use crates like panic-probe to provide a fallback mechanism that logs the panic message before halting. You can configure this handler to run specific code, such as printing via defmt or writing to an external flash memory region, instead of just stopping instantly.
I see how useful that is for debugging during development, but I'm wondering if there's a way to make panics completely silent so the device keeps running? Like maybe catching them and doing nothing?

In theory you could write a #[panic_handler] that does no-op logging, but this is generally discouraged in production because it hides bugs. If your code panics silently, you won't know what went wrong until you can't reproduce the issue later. Most serious embedded projects prefer to log panics via defmt or another logging crate so you can catch them during testing and verify that your logic doesn't actually panic under real-world conditions.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how exactly the stack unwinds works differently here compared to my web apps. Does it just stop at a certain point?

When you have #[panic_handler] configured in no_std environments, the panic signal is intercepted before full stack unwind occurs. The handler receives control and can execute code like logging or resetting memory before terminating. This means your application doesn't necessarily crash immediately; instead it goes through a defined cleanup routine. In contrast, web frameworks often rely on higher-level error handling logic that prevents panics from reaching the runtime at all.
Okay so I'm thinking about how to implement this myself for my next project. Should I use panic-probe or is there something better? And what exactly does defmt do?

panic-prove is a great starting point because it provides a simple way to log panics before halting, and you can easily customize the logging behavior using defmt. Defmt is a zero-cost logging format that allows you to serialize panic messages without allocating memory on the heap, which is crucial in resource-constrained environments. This makes defmt particularly useful when combined with panic-probe because it ensures your logs are small and don't impact performance.

I'm starting to understand how valuable zero-cost logging is for my project, but I'm wondering if there's a downside to using defmt compared to standard println? Like maybe the code gets harder to read?

Defmt does require writing custom serialization logic for your types, which means you need to implement traits like Serialize for each struct you want to log. This adds some boilerplate code and requires careful type design, but it trades complexity for performance. Standard println uses the standard library's formatting machinery which allocates memory and strings, whereas defmt avoids all of that by using a custom protocol designed specifically for embedded systems where heap allocation is undesirable.
I guess I'm still not sure how to actually set up the panic handler in my code. Does it go at the top of main or somewhere else?

You define #[panic_handler] before your main function, typically outside any other functions like init or setup. This ensures that if a panic occurs during initialization or runtime, it will be caught immediately rather than unwinding the entire stack. The handler can then execute code to log information via defmt and optionally reset memory regions before halting execution.

Alright so I think I get the big picture now: define the handler early, use defmt for logging without allocations, but I'm still curious about what happens if you panic during initialization. Does it even matter?

Panic during initialization is critical because it prevents your system from booting at all unless you have a recovery mechanism in place. Without a #[panic_handler], the device will never start running your application code, which means no logs or data collection can occur. This is why panic-probe and defmt are so valuable—they allow you to detect initialization failures early while still getting useful diagnostic information before the system completely stops.

Thanks for walking me through this! I think I finally understand how panics behave differently in no_std environments compared to my web apps, and how panic-probe helps catch them gracefully. One last question: can you show me a simplified example of setting up defmt logging?

Sure, here's a basic setup where you define your panic handler early using #[panic_handler] and configure it to log via defmt before halting. You'll need to enable the defmt feature in your Cargo.toml and implement traits for your types so they can be serialized without heap allocation.

I'm getting a compile error when I try to enable the panic handler for my microcontroller. The message says there's an unexpected token at the end of the file, and it seems like my panic hook isn't being recognized by the compiler even though I have #[panic_handler] in my code.

The issue is that you're likely trying to use a standard panic handler from the library crate, which allocates memory and expects `main` to return an `Option<i32>`. In no_std environments with bare-metal tools like cortex-m, the panic hook must be registered using the `#[panic_handler]` attribute directly in your entry point. You should define a function that takes `(core::panic::PanicInfo, &mut core::fmt::Formatter<'static>)` and returns nothing. If you're using defmt for logging, ensure you're not expecting any return value from the panic hook itself.

Okay, I tried defining a function with the right signature but it still won't compile. It looks like my function isn't being recognized as the panic handler even though I used the #[panic_handler] attribute correctly.

The problem is probably that you're trying to use `#[no_mangle]` or some other visibility modifier incorrectly, or perhaps your function isn't in the same crate as where the panic handler macro resolves. In no_std, the panic hook must be defined at the top level of your entry function so the linker knows exactly what to expect. Also, make sure you're not accidentally returning anything from that panic handler function since it needs to return void implicitly.
I managed to get the panic handler registered but now I'm seeing a warning about unused import for core::fmt::Formatter. Is this normal?

Yes, that's expected behavior when using defmt logging in no_std environments since you're not actually using the standard fmt module. The compiler sees an unused import because defmt bypasses the Formatter trait entirely. You can suppress this warning by adding `#![allow(unused_imports)]` at the top of your file or simply ignore it since defmt messages are being logged correctly without going through the standard formatter.
Thanks, that cleared it up. One last thing though - if I want to log more detailed panic information beyond just the message string, is there a way to do that with defmt?

Yes, you can use defmt's formatting capabilities similar to printf but optimized for embedded systems. You'll need to implement custom types using the DefmtDisplay trait if you want to log complex data structures like arrays or structs without allocating heap memory. The panic hook can then call `defmt::assert!(message)` before unwinding, allowing you to capture stack traces and variable states at runtime without triggering a full allocation.

I'm building a tool to automate repetitive tasks on an embedded board using no_std. I noticed that if my code panics, the device just stops and hangs. How do I configure a panic handler so it doesn't crash?

In a no_std environment without a full runtime like Rust's standard library, you cannot rely on unwinding or printing errors to the console. You must define your own #[panic_handler] macro that intercepts any panic and logs it via defmt before halting the processor. Think of this handler as the emergency brake system for your application; when something goes wrong instead of letting the car crash into a wall, the brake engages, records the speed and direction (the panic message), then safely stops the vehicle.
That analogy helps. So I need to write a function that takes a string and writes it to defmt? Does this require specific traits or crates?

Yes, you will need the panic-probe crate which provides utilities for generating panic messages without allocating heap memory. The panic handler function must implement the core trait required by the compiler to handle panics correctly in a bare-metal context. You should also ensure your logging implementation uses defmt's debug traits so that the message is formatted efficiently at compile time.
I want to know exactly how to implement this without using any heap allocation, since my microcontroller has very limited RAM. Can you show me a basic implementation?

Here is the structure of your panic handler function. It takes the panic message as a string slice and uses defmt::format! to log it, then calls halt() to stop execution forever. The key here is using no-alloc panic messages so you don't spend precious stack space on temporary allocations.
Wait, I think I misunderstood the panic message part. Is it a static string or does it come from runtime?

The panic message itself is generated at compile time through macros like panic!() which produce a const string slice. Your handler receives that pre-computed string and passes it to defmt for logging. This ensures the entire flow from panic generation to log output happens without any dynamic memory operations.
What if I want to send a signal to another core or just write to an LED instead of halting immediately?

You can customize your panic handler function to perform custom actions before calling halt. Instead of stopping immediately, you could configure the handler to log the message via defmt and then toggle a specific GPIO pin as an external signal. This gives you more control over how the system reacts to unexpected errors.
I'm still concerned about stack overflow when my panic handler tries to format a large string. How do I avoid that?

To prevent stack overflow, ensure your panic message is small and use defmt's compile-time formatting capabilities. Avoid passing large arrays or strings directly into the logging function; instead, serialize only essential parts of the error data. This keeps your runtime footprint minimal while still capturing enough information for debugging.

I'm following along with that talk on embedded Rust and I'm confused about the difference between #[panic_handler] and a regular panic. In my scripting language, does panicking actually stop execution like in C?

That's a great question because it trips up many newcomers. A regular panic inside a library function just unwinds the stack and returns an error to the caller, but #[panic_handler] is special since it defines what happens when a panic occurs in the main application entry point itself. In no_std environments, you can't rely on the OS to catch that signal automatically, so you must explicitly implement this trait using #[panic_handler] or derive PanicHandler. If you don't set up #[panic_handler] properly, your device might just reset or enter an undefined state instead of printing useful logs.

Ah okay, so I thought #[panic_handler] was just a macro for catching panics in regular functions. But since my chip doesn't have a full OS, how do I actually log the panic message without allocating memory?

You're right to notice that distinction. In no_std, you can't use #[panic_handler] for just any function because it requires a specific trait implementation and the panic itself must be handled by the runtime. For logging without heap allocation, I'd recommend using defmt with the panic-probe crate which provides a no-alloc panic message format. The panic-probe crate lets you define custom handlers that write to UART or other peripherals instead of crashing. You should avoid #[panic_handler] for library code and only use it in your main application where you control the startup behavior.

Wait, so I shouldn't even try to catch panics from helper functions? That feels dangerous if those functions are called during critical initialization. Is there a way to make #[panic_handler] work for those cases without full no_std support?

Exactly, and that's why panic handling is usually reserved for the main application entry point in embedded Rust. Helper functions should just let panics propagate up so they can be caught at the top level where you control #[panic_handler]. If you really need to handle panics inside a library function, you'd need std::panic::catch_unwind instead of #[panic_handler], but that requires some standard library features which aren't always available in pure no_std. The panic-probe crate specifically avoids this by letting you define handlers that work at the application level without needing full std support.
So if I'm using panic-probe with defmt, do I still need to manually implement #[panic_handler] or does it handle everything automatically?

The panic-probe crate provides the panic handler implementation for you, but you still need to configure where that handler runs in your application. You don't implement #[panic_handler] yourself directly; instead, panic-probe sets up a custom panic handler that uses defmt to log messages without allocation. The key is ensuring your #[panic_handler] trait implementation is properly wired up so the panic message gets written to your UART or other output device before any reset occurs.
One more thing, what happens if I accidentally call a function that panics during boot and my #[panic_handler] isn't set up yet? Will the system just crash immediately?

Yes, absolutely. If your #[panic_handler] isn't configured before the panic occurs, there's no place for the panic message to go and the processor might enter an undefined state or reset unexpectedly. This is why panic-probe crate specifically emphasizes setting up handlers early in the boot sequence. The defmt logging happens inside the panic handler itself, so without that handler being active first, you lose all visibility into what went wrong during critical initialization phases.

I am getting frustrated trying to understand why a panic just halts the program completely instead of printing an error message like it does on x86_62 Linux. I thought panics were for debugging, not for crashing embedded devices immediately.

You are absolutely right to be confused because standard Rust assumes you have an allocator and a runtime that can catch exceptions. In no_std environments without a panic hook set up, the panic implementation is just an unhandled exception that causes immediate termination. When you use #[panic_handler] in your startup code, you define how those panics are handled before the main function runs.
But I don't want my device to die when I accidentally call a wrong function during boot. Is there any way to recover from a panic without using a full operating system or dynamic memory allocation?

Yes, you can use defmt logging and the panic-probe crate which provide no-alloc panic messages that print to your hardware serial interface instead of crashing. These are designed specifically for microcontrollers where you cannot afford the overhead of malloc or a full OS.
Okay so I just need to implement #[panic_handler] in my main function and call defmt? But what if I don't have an allocator available at all?
Exactly, that is why panic-probe crate exists as a no-alloc panic handler. It allows you to log panic messages without allocating any heap memory by using static buffers or direct hardware registers.
So if I use panic-probe instead of defmt directly, does it give me better performance since there is no allocation involved?

Yes, panic-probe is specifically optimized for zero-allocation scenarios on constrained devices. It avoids the runtime overhead of standard panic handlers by using compile-time constants and direct memory access patterns.
Wait so I shouldn't even use #[panic_handler] then? Can you clarify when to use defmt versus panic-probe?

Use defmt for rich logging with minimal runtime overhead once your boot sequence completes, but panic-probe is better for the initial startup phase where you want immediate feedback without any allocation. You can combine them by having panic-probe handle panics during initialization and defmt handle panics in your main logic.
I've got a working #[panic_handler] that just calls halt() when panic happens, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle this in an embedded context without using full std.

In no_std environments you typically don't want unwinding the stack or calling functions from std::panic. Instead, you define your own panic handler that logs via defmt and then calls a custom halt function. You can use the panic-probe crate to generate a minimal panic message without any allocations.
That sounds good but I'm not sure how to integrate it properly. My current setup uses println! which allocates and prints to stdout, but in embedded we want something faster.

You should avoid println! because it requires std::io and allocations. Instead, use defmt logging directly from the panic handler. The panic-probe crate can generate a compact string that you pass to defmt_send or similar macros in your panic_handler function.
I tried using println! but it crashes my target because stdout isn't available at runtime. Is there a way to make panic messages work without full std?

Yes, you can use no-alloc panic message generation with panic-probe and then log via defmt::debug! in your #[panic_handler]. This avoids any heap allocation or I/O calls until the very end when you actually halt.
So basically instead of unwinding, I'm just logging once and then stopping? But what if there are multiple panics happening in my code?

Correct. In a no_std context with #[panic_handler], each panic triggers your custom handler exactly once per panic event. You log the message via defmt and then halt. There's no automatic unwinding chain like in std, so you control how many times it fires.
I see now but I'm still confused about why Rust allows panics at all if they just halt the program. Isn't that bad for safety?

In embedded systems with no_std, panics are often intentional failure modes like missing configuration or invalid state. The #[panic_handler] gives you control to log critical info before halting rather than silently crashing.
Ah okay so instead of just dying immediately, I can use panic-probe + defmt to get meaningful logs. But what if my panic handler itself panics?

You need to ensure your #[panic_handler] doesn't panic internally unless you want double halts. Usually you design it to only log and halt, avoiding any condition that could trigger another panic in the same process.
Makes sense! So my new plan is: use panic-probe for compact messages, defmt for logging, and a custom halt function instead of std::process::exit. That avoids allocations entirely.

Exactly. You'll define #[panic_handler] in your main crate, call panic-probe to generate the message, log via defmt, then invoke a simple halt function that sets a flag or calls an external reset routine.

Hey, I've been reading about panic handlers for embedded systems but honestly still don't get why we need #[panic_handler] when panics just stop the program. We're building a tool to automate repetitive tasks on our board and I'm worried if my code panics it'll just die without any trace.

That's actually exactly right, that's one of the hardest parts for junior devs to grasp. In no_std environments like bare-metal you can't rely on the OS to catch unhandled panics so #[panic_handler] becomes mandatory because it intercepts the panic before the stack unwinds completely. Without this trait implementation your program halts immediately when a panic occurs, which is why we use crates like panic-probe or defmt to log the message instead of letting it crash silently.
Wait so if I just print println! in my error function it won't help? Like isn't that what panics are supposed to do? Or am I missing something about how the panic mechanism works at runtime?

You're missing a key detail: regular println! calls alloc memory which is forbidden on many microcontrollers, so you need no-alloc panic messages instead. The panic handler replaces your default implementation with custom logic that logs via defmt or similar low-level logging traits without touching the heap. It essentially hooks into the panic hook to capture the message and write it directly to flash or UART before resetting.

Oh okay so I can't use println! at all then? But what if I have a complex error like deserialization failure in serde_json::Value handling? How do I actually get that specific string into the panic handler without bloating my code?

You don't need to rewrite your entire logic, you just wrap the panic point. When serde_json::Value fails to parse and triggers a panic, your #[panic_handler] intercepts it and formats the message using defmt which has zero allocations. You can pass context like the deserialized value through the panic hook argument so the handler logs exactly what went wrong without needing extra memory.

That sounds useful but I'm still confused about how the panic-probe crate fits in here since we're not really doing probes, just logging. Does it replace #[panic_handler] or work alongside it? And what about stack unwinding if the panic happens deep inside my code?

panic-probe is actually a fallback for when you can't implement your own #[panic_handler], so they don't replace each other but serve different needs. If you implement #[panic_handler] with defmt logging, you bypass the need for probes entirely since your custom handler does the actual work of capturing and writing the panic message. Stack unwinding is usually disabled in embedded contexts to save space, so even if a panic occurs deep in your code it won't blow up the stack as long your #[panic_handler] doesn't require full unwind semantics.

Gotcha, so I should just skip stack unwinding and rely on defmt for my logs. But wait, what happens if there are multiple panics in sequence? Will the second one get caught by the same handler or does it reset everything?

The #[panic_handler] runs every time a panic occurs so yes, sequential panics will all be caught individually unless you manually clear the log buffer. Since no_std systems often have limited flash space, you might want to implement a circular buffer in your panic handler to overwrite old messages instead of accumulating them indefinitely.

Perfect, that makes sense for our constraints. So my plan is: remove println!, add #[panic_handler] with defmt logging, and disable stack unwinding? Is there anything else I should watch out for before deploying this to production?

Just verify your panic messages are compile-time constants or use safe string formatting traits like format! on the message itself. Also ensure your #[panic_handler] doesn't introduce any allocations during runtime since those will still crash the system even if you catch the initial panic.

Thanks for breaking that down, I think I finally get how the pieces fit together now. One last question though: what exactly is defmt and why do people keep saying it's better than println? Is it just faster or does it actually avoid something else entirely?

Defmt is a custom logging format designed specifically for embedded systems that avoids string parsing overhead completely. Unlike println! which requires building strings before output, defmt encodes values into binary and decodes them at runtime with minimal CPU cycles, making it ideal for tight loops or real-time automation tasks where every cycle counts.
Wow, so no string allocation at all? That's exactly what I needed to hear. Time to start refactoring my panic handling code then!
Exactly, and remember to test your #[panic_handler] thoroughly since embedded panics are hard to reproduce without hardware triggers. Good luck with the automation tool!
